items.title: How citizens’ experience of democracy can actually pave the way to democratic backsliding
items.author: Stephan Lewandowsky
items.content: Why do many citizens of Western Europe appear complacent about their societies’ democratic backsliding? One explanation is the effect of personal experience on risk perception: a stable democratic past lulls humans into a false sense of security.

items.title: Rewards bias self-evaluations of ability
items.author: Leor M. Hackel
items.content: People often receive rewards for good performance, but what happens when rewards do not reflect ability? Two behavioral studies suggest that rewards can impact how we evaluate our own ability, above and beyond the impact of actual performance.

items.title: Escalating risk-taking is linked to emotional habituation
items.author: Tali Sharot
items.content: Using virtual reality to simulate physical risk, we show that over time, risk-taking escalates while emotional responses to risk habituate. The greater the emotional habituation, the steeper the risk escalation.

items.title: Using machine learning to predict persecutory beliefs based on aetiological models of delusions identified in a systematic literature search
items.author: Tania M. Lincoln
items.content: Using machine learning, this study revealed that predictors from 51 theoretical models of delusions explain only 31% of the variance in persecutory beliefs, raising questions about current theories and pointing to gaps in understanding the specific aetiology of delusions.

items.title: Intentional binding effect depends on conscious access to the sensory consequences of action
items.author: Howard C. Nusbaum
items.content: This Registered Report finds that an implicit measure of subjective agency – intentional binding, where the perceived time of an action is biased toward that of its consequence – is altered when the consequence is masked from conscious awareness.

items.title: Humans and LLMs rate deliberation as superior to intuition on complex reasoning tasks
items.author: Matthieu Raoelison
items.content: People and LLMs evaluate deliberative reasoning more favorably than intuitive thinking—even when both yield accurate results. This preference appears to be intuitive itself and has implications for how we assess others’ and AI advise.

items.title: The order of task decisions and confidence ratings has little effect on metacognition
items.author: Kazuhisa Shibata
items.content: Decisions and confidence ratings are crucial to metacognition research. A concern is whether the order in which first and second order ratings are collected may affect results. This Registered Report finds order has little effect on metacognitive efficiency.

items.title: Internal uncertainty impacts social information use in risky choice across adolescence
items.author: Wouter van den Bos
items.content: Adolescents’ choices are influenced by others. A social risky choice experiment and Bayesian modelling reveal that age differences in internal uncertainty, being unsure how to choose, relate to differences in susceptibility to social influence.
